heb.paragraph: The verb indicates panicked flight, not a leisurely departure. Jonah does not merely decline the commission; he bolts in the opposite direction. The narrator emphasizes the absurdity: one cannot flee from the presence of the omnipresent God (Ps 139:7-12).

heb.gloss: from the presence of the LORD
heb.paragraph: The phrase occurs twice in v. 3, underscoring Jonah's deliberate intent. In prophetic literature, standing 'before the LORD' (lipnê YHWH) denotes prophetic service; fleeing 'from the presence of' (millipnê) is its exact reversal — an abdication of calling.

refs.note: The only other biblical reference to Jonah son of Amittai, where he delivers a nationalistic oracle of territorial restoration under Jeroboam II.
The impossibility of fleeing God's presence — a theological premise that Jonah's narrative enacts dramatically.
Cain went out 'from the presence of the LORD' — the same phrase used of Jonah, linking flight from God's presence to the primal narrative of rebellion.

notes.note: Jonah is a historical figure confirmed by 2 Kings 14:25, not a fictional character. He prophesied during the reign of Jeroboam II, a period of Israelite prosperity but spiritual decline. God's command to preach against Nineveh, the capital of Israel's most feared enemy, explains Jonah's reluctance.

Nineveh was the great city of the Assyrian Empire, known for its extreme cruelty in warfare. Jonah's refusal reflects not cowardice but theological objection: he feared God would show mercy to Israel's oppressor (cf. 4:2).

Tarshish was likely in modern Spain, representing the farthest known point west. Jonah paid his own fare, attempting to use his resources to escape divine appointment. Every step away from God's will is a step downward.

Calvin observes that God's sovereignty is displayed even in the giving of the command. The LORD does not negotiate with his prophet but issues a direct mandate. The wickedness of Nineveh has 'come up' before God, echoing the language used of Sodom (Gen 18:20-21), yet unlike Sodom, Nineveh will receive the chance to repent.

Calvin notes the absurdity of Jonah's flight: the prophet knew better than anyone that God is omnipresent, yet terror and resentment drove him to act against his own theology. This is the anatomy of disobedience — sin makes fools of the wise.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The formula 'the word of the LORD came to' (wayehi devar-YHWH el) is the standard prophetic commission formula, identical to that used for Elijah, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel. It establishes Jonah's calling as genuine and authoritative.

The Hebrew text uses 'from the presence of the LORD' (millipne YHWH) twice in this verse for emphatic effect. The preposition min ('from') combined with lipne ('the face/presence of') indicates departure from the sphere of prophetic service, not merely a geographical relocation.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart emphasizes that the book's opening mirrors the standard prophetic call formula but immediately subverts expectations: where the prophet normally accepts (however reluctantly), Jonah flees. The command to 'cry against' (qera aleiha) Nineveh uses the same verb later used for Jonah's preaching in 3:2, creating an inclusio between the two commissions. The phrase 'their wickedness has come up before me' employs courtroom language: Nineveh's sin has reached the divine tribunal.

Stuart notes that Tarshish (likely Tartessus in Spain) was a byword for the remotest western destination. The narrative structure emphasizes descent: Jonah 'went down' to Joppa, 'went down' into the ship, and will later 'go down' into the hold and finally into the sea. This descent motif tracks the prophet's spiritual trajectory until the reversal in chapter 2.

hist.context: The opening verses establish the central tension of the book: God commands, Jonah disobeys. Unlike every other prophetic call narrative in the Hebrew Bible, the prophet's response is flight rather than protest (cf. Moses, Jeremiah, Isaiah). Tarshish represents the western edge of the known world — as far from Nineveh (to the east) as one could travel. The theological irony is thick: Jonah flees from the God whose sovereignty the book will demonstrate extends over sea, storm, fish, plant, worm, wind, and pagan empire alike.

heb.paragraph: The same verb (tūl) is used of God hurling the storm (v. 4), the sailors hurling cargo (v. 5), and the sailors hurling Jonah (v. 15). The repetition creates a chain of causation: God acts, creation responds, and finally the sailors must act on Jonah.

heb.gloss: fast asleep / in a deep sleep
heb.paragraph: A rare word suggesting a death-like torpor (cf. Judg 4:21; Ps 76:7). While creation rages and pagans pray, the prophet of YHWH is unconscious below deck — an image of spiritual oblivion.

refs.note: A psalm describing God sending storms at sea and calming them when sailors cry out — the very pattern enacted in Jonah 1.
Jesus asleep during a storm while disciples panic, with the same elements reversed: Jesus commands the sea, and the disciples ask 'Who is this?'

notes.note: God 'sent' (literally 'hurled') a great wind. The storm was not natural but supernatural — a direct divine intervention to halt the prophet's flight. God's sovereignty over nature is a consistent theme throughout this book.

The sailors each cried out to their own god, showing the polytheistic context of the ancient Mediterranean world. Meanwhile the prophet of the one true God was sleeping. The irony is devastating and intentional.
Lot-casting was a common ancient practice for discerning divine will. Proverbs 16:33 affirms that even lots are governed by God's sovereign purposes.
Jonah's theological confession is impeccable: YHWH is the God of heaven who made sea and land. Yet his behavior flatly contradicts his creed — a warning against orthodoxy divorced from obedience.
Calvin draws attention to God's governance of natural forces as instruments of his will. The storm is not blind fate but purposeful pursuit. God will not allow his servant to succeed in rebellion.

Calvin remarks that Jonah's confession condemns himself: if YHWH made the sea, then the sea is his domain, and no ship can carry a man beyond his reach. The prophet's theology refutes the prophet's actions.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb hēṭl ('hurled') personifies God's action, treating the wind as a weapon deliberately aimed. The verb recurs three times in this chapter (vv. 4, 5, 15), forming a thematic chain connecting divine initiative, human response, and resolution.

The Hebrew nirdam ('deep sleep') is used elsewhere only of Sisera's death-sleep (Judg 4:21) and God's judgment sleep (Ps 76:7 [Eng. 76:6]). The word choice suggests Jonah is spiritually comatose, not merely tired.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart highlights the narrative's careful use of the keyword 'great' (gadol): great city (v. 2), great wind (v. 4), great storm (v. 4), great fear (vv. 10, 16). The word occurs fourteen times in the book, tying together divine actions and human responses. The captain's command to Jonah ('Arise, call out') uses the same two verbs as God's original command in v. 2, creating a structural echo.

Stuart observes that the lot-casting scene functions as a trial: the lots identify the guilty party, and Jonah's interrogation follows the form of ancient legal proceedings. His confession in v. 9 is both theologically correct and dramatically ironic — the man who claims to 'fear' (yare) YHWH is the one running from him.

hist.context: The storm scene is saturated with irony. Pagan sailors pray to their gods while God's prophet sleeps. The captain's command to Jonah ('Get up and call on your god!') echoes God's original command ('Get up and go to Nineveh and call out against it'), using the identical Hebrew verb qum ('arise/get up'). The lot-casting reveals Jonah, and his confession — 'I am a Hebrew, and I worship the LORD, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land' — is orthodox theology spoken by a man whose actions contradict every word of it.

heb.paragraph: The text says 'great fish' (dag gadol), not 'whale.' The word dag is the generic term for any large aquatic creature. The narrator's emphasis is on divine appointment, not zoological classification: God 'provided' (manah) the fish, using a verb of sovereign orchestration that recurs for the vine, worm, and wind in chapter 4.

This verb appears four times in the book (1:17; 4:6, 7, 8), each time with God as subject. It describes sovereign provision of created agents — fish, plant, worm, wind — to accomplish divine purposes. The word implies deliberate assignment, not coincidence.

refs.note: Jesus identifies Jonah's three days in the fish as a type of his own three days in the tomb — the foundational NT use of Jonah as Christological sign.
A lament psalm about drowning in deep waters, sharing vocabulary and imagery with Jonah's prayer in chapter 2.

notes.note: Jonah's willingness to be thrown overboard rather than repent and go to Nineveh reveals the depth of his stubbornness. He would rather die than obey God's command to show mercy to pagans.

The sailors' prayer to YHWH, their sacrifices, and their vows represent genuine conversion. These pagans respond to God more readily than his own prophet. This anticipates the theme of chapter 3, where Nineveh repents.
The great fish was a divinely appointed means of preservation, not punishment. God prepared the fish specifically for this moment. Jesus affirmed the historicity of this event (Matt 12:40).

Calvin notes with characteristic precision that the sailors' fear progressed from terror of the storm to reverent fear of the LORD. Their sacrifices and vows indicate genuine worship, however imperfect. God grants knowledge of himself even to those outside the covenant community.
Calvin insists on the literal, miraculous nature of the fish. To allegorize the event is to undermine the entire narrative and, by extension, Christ's own use of it as a sign of resurrection.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The sailors' prayer 'O LORD, please do not let us die for taking this man's life' uses the divine name YHWH, not a generic 'god.' By this point in the narrative, the pagan sailors have moved from polytheistic prayer (v. 5) to addressing Israel's God by his covenant name.

The verb manah ('appointed/provided') is a key theological term in Jonah, appearing four times (1:17; 4:6, 7, 8). Each usage demonstrates God's sovereign control over the created order: he appoints a fish, a plant, a worm, and a wind. Nothing in the narrative is accidental.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart reads this scene as the book's first conversion narrative: the sailors move from pagan polytheism through theological questioning to Yahwistic worship in a single episode. Their sacrifices and vows (v. 16) use standard OT covenant language. The narrative thus anticipates the Ninevite conversion in chapter 3 and challenges any Israelite assumption that pagans are beyond God's reach.

Stuart argues that the three-day period is a standard Hebrew idiom for a significant duration of crisis and transition (cf. Gen 22:4; Exod 3:18; Hos 6:2). The fish functions as both tomb and womb — a place of death and rebirth — preparing the prophet for his renewed commission in 3:1.

hist.context: The scene reaches its climax as the sailors — with great reluctance — hurl Jonah into the sea. The irony intensifies: pagan sailors show more moral sensitivity than the Hebrew prophet, praying to YHWH and offering sacrifices after the sea calms. Their 'great fear of the LORD' (v. 16) contrasts with Jonah's claimed fear of the LORD (v. 9). The great fish is not punishment but preservation: God rescues Jonah from drowning to give him a second chance.

lit.note: Chapter 1 follows a descent pattern: Jonah goes down to Joppa, down into the ship, down into the hold, and will descend into the sea and the fish. Each step downward corresponds to increasing distance from his calling and increasing proximity to death.

pairs.label: A
pairs.top: God commissions Jonah to go to Nineveh; Jonah flees in the opposite direction (1:1–3)
pairs.bottom: God commissions Jonah a second time; Jonah obeys and goes to Nineveh (3:1–3)

pairs.label: B
pairs.top: Pagan sailors fear, pray, and sacrifice — they turn to the LORD (1:4–16)
pairs.bottom: Pagan Ninevites fear, fast, and repent — they turn from violence (3:4–10)

pairs.label: C
pairs.top: Jonah in the belly of the fish — gives thanks for deliverance from death (2:1–10)
pairs.bottom: Jonah outside the city — angry about God’s mercy and wishes for death (4:1–4)

center.label: X
center.text: The mirror’s hinge: pagans consistently respond rightly to God while the prophet consistently responds wrongly. In the first half, Jonah sinks and is saved; in the second half, Jonah is right-side-up but spiritually upside-down. The book’s question is God’s final question: “Should I not have concern for Nineveh?” (4:5–11).

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates at 9: God controls wind, sea, lots, and fish. Mission scores 8 as the theme of prophetic commission and flight drives the plot. Judgment at 7 reflects both the storm and the threat against Nineveh.

hebtext.note: The verb indicates panicked flight, not a leisurely departure. Jonah does not merely decline the commission; he bolts in the opposite direction. The narrator emphasizes the absurdity: one cannot flee from the presence of the omnipresent God (Ps 139:7-12).

hebtext.gloss: from the presence of the LORD
hebtext.note: The phrase occurs twice in v. 3, underscoring Jonah's deliberate intent. In prophetic literature, standing 'before the LORD' (lipnê YHWH) denotes prophetic service; fleeing 'from the presence of' (millipnê) is its exact reversal — an abdication of calling.

The same verb (tūl) is used of God hurling the storm (v. 4), the sailors hurling cargo (v. 5), and the sailors hurling Jonah (v. 15). The repetition creates a chain of causation: God acts, creation responds, and finally the sailors must act on Jonah.

hebtext.gloss: fast asleep / in a deep sleep
hebtext.note: A rare word suggesting a death-like torpor (cf. Judg 4:21; Ps 76:7). While creation rages and pagans pray, the prophet of YHWH is unconscious below deck — an image of spiritual oblivion.

The text says 'great fish' (dag gadol), not 'whale.' The word dag is the generic term for any large aquatic creature. The narrator's emphasis is on divine appointment, not zoological classification: God 'provided' (manah) the fish, using a verb of sovereign orchestration that recurs for the vine, worm, and wind in chapter 4.

This verb appears four times in the book (1:17; 4:6, 7, 8), each time with God as subject. It describes sovereign provision of created agents — fish, plant, worm, wind — to accomplish divine purposes. The word implies deliberate assignment, not coincidence.

ppl.name: Jonah
ppl.text: The opening verses establish the central tension of the book: God commands, Jonah disobeys…

ppl.name: Jeroboam II
ppl.text: He prophesied during the reign of Jeroboam II, a period of Israelite prosperity but spiritual decline…

ppl.name: Jesus
ppl.text: Jesus affirmed the historicity of this event (Matt 12:40)…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Jonah Flees from the LORD

translations.text: All the sailors were afraid and each cried out to his own god. And they threw the cargo into the sea to lighten the ship. But Jonah had gone below deck, where he lay down and fell into a deep sleep.

Then the mariners were afraid, and each cried out to his god. And they hurled the cargo that was in the ship into the sea to lighten it for them. But Jonah had gone down into the inner part of the ship and had lain down and was fast asleep.

But Jonah ran away from the Lord and headed for Tarshish. He went down to Joppa, where he found a ship bound for that port. After paying the fare, he went aboard and sailed for Tarshish to flee from the Lord.

But Jonah rose to flee to Tarshish from the presence of the LORD. He went down to Joppa and found a ship going to Tarshish. So he paid the fare and went down into it, to go with them to Tarshish, away from the presence of the LORD.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Jonah is a historical figure confirmed by 2 Kings 14:25, not a fictional character. He prophesied during the reign of Jeroboam II, a period of Israelite prosperity but spiritual decline. God's command…"

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin observes that God's sovereignty is displayed even in the giving of the command. The LORD does not negotiate with his prophet but issues a direct mandate. The wickedness of Nineveh has 'come up'…"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The formula 'the word of the LORD came to' (wayehi devar-YHWH el) is the standard prophetic commission formula, identical to that used for Elijah, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel. It establishes Jonah's calling…"

debate: The formula 'the word of the LORD came to' (wayehi devar-YHWH el) is the standard prophetic commission formula, identical to that used for Elijah, Jer…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Jonah Flees from the LORD within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: The only other biblical reference to Jonah son of Amittai, where he delivers a nationalistic oracle of territorial restoration under Jeroboam II.
The impossibility of fleeing God's presence — a theological premise that Jonah's narrative enacts dramatically.
Cain went out 'from the presence of the LORD' — the same phrase used of Jonah, linking flight from God's presence to the primal narrative of rebellion.
A psalm describing God sending storms at sea and calming them when sailors cry out — the very pattern enacted in Jonah 1.
Jesus asleep during a storm while disciples panic, with the same elements reversed: Jesus commands the sea, and the disciples ask 'Who is this?'
Jesus identifies Jonah's three days in the fish as a type of his own three days in the tomb — the foundational NT use of Jonah as Christological sign.

coaching.tip: God says 'go to Nineveh' (east); Jonah flees to Tarshish (west) — the opposite direction, the edge of the known world. But notice verse 3: 'away from the presence of the LORD.' Jonah isn't just avoiding Nineveh; he's trying to escape God. This is impossible, as Psalm 139 makes clear, but the attempt itself reveals what's driving him: anger at God's character, not fear of Nineveh.

The pagan sailors pray, cast lots, sacrifice, and make vows — all while Jonah sleeps in the hold. The prophet of Israel is less responsive to the storm than pagans who 'each cried out to his god.' This ironic reversal continues throughout: Nineveh will repent while Jonah sulks. The book is asking Israel: who are the real outsiders?

Jonah offers a bizarre prayer: 'Throw me into the sea.' Is this repentance or suicide? Perhaps both. He knows he's the cause, accepts the consequence, yet still won't simply obey. Even his surrender is stubborn. The fish becomes both judgment and mercy — the same pattern God will offer Nineveh, and the same tension Jonah can't accept.
